Let $f(x) = \begin{cases} x^n \sin \frac{1}{x} & , \ x \neq 0 \\ 0 & , \ x = 0 \end{cases}$
Such that $f(x)$ is continuous at $x = 0$; $f'(0)$ is real and finite; and $\lim_{x \to 0} f'(x)$ does not exist. This holds true for which of the following values of $n$ ?

options: $f(x)$ is non-differentiable at exactly three points

text: Let $f : R \to R$ be a function, such that $|f(x)| \le x^{4n}, \ n \in N \ \forall x \in R$ then $f(x)$ is :

options: $\lim_{x \to 1} g(x)$ exists, but $g(x)$ is not continuous at $x = 1$

text: Let the function $f$ be defined by $f(x) = \begin{cases} p + qx + x^2 & , \ x < 2 \\ 2px + 3qx^2 & , \ x \ge 2 \end{cases}$. Then :
options: If $p = -2, q = 1$, then $f(x)$ is continuous in $R$

text: Let $f(x) = |2x - 9| + |2x| + |2x + 9|$. Which of the following are true ?

options: $\text{sgn}(f(x))$ is non-differentiable at exactly one point

text: Let $f(x) = (x^2 - 3x + 2)(x^2 + 3x + 2)$ and $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ satisfy $\alpha < \beta < \gamma$ are the roots of $f'(x) = 0$ then which of the following is/are correct ($[\cdot]$ denotes greatest integer function) ?
